hook: You already know how to find a location on a number line — every number has exactly one spot. But what if you need to describe a location on a flat surface, like the position of a piece on a chessboard or a pin dropped on a map? A single number isn't enough anymore. How do you use two numbers together to pinpoint any location on a plane?
concept: The coordinate plane is a flat grid formed by two perpendicular number lines crossing at a point called the origin. The horizontal line is called the $x$-axis and the vertical line is called the $y$-axis. Every location on the plane is described by an ordered pair $(x, y)$, where $x$ tells you how far to move left or right from the origin and $y$ tells you how far to move up or down.
anatomy: An ordered pair $(x, y)$ has two components: the first number is the $x$-coordinate (also called the abscissa), which measures horizontal distance from the origin — positive values go right, negative values go left. The second number is the $y$-coordinate (also called the ordinate), which measures vertical distance — positive values go up, negative values go down. The origin itself is the point $(0, 0)$ where both axes meet, and the axes divide the plane into four regions called quadrants, numbered I through IV counterclockwise starting from the upper-right.

worked_example.description_latex: We start at the origin $(0, 0)$, which is always our reference point. The ordered pair $(3, 2)$ tells us exactly two moves to make: first handle the $x$-coordinate, then the $y$-coordinate.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Step 1: Move } 3 \text{ units to the right along the } x\text{-axis}
worked_example.description_latex: Because the $x$-coordinate is $3$ (a positive number), we move 3 units to the right from the origin. If it were negative we would move left, so the sign of the $x$-coordinate always controls our horizontal direction.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Step 2: Move } 2 \text{ units up, parallel to the } y\text{-axis}
worked_example.description_latex: From the spot we landed after the horizontal move, we now move 2 units upward because the $y$-coordinate is $+2$. It is important to make the vertical move from where you stopped horizontally — not from the origin again.
The point is now plotted, and we can confirm it sits in Quadrant I because both coordinates are positive. Checking the quadrant is a quick way to verify you moved in the right directions.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Plot the point } B = (-4, 3) \text{ and name its quadrant}
partial_example.description_latex: We begin at the origin. Our ordered pair is $(-4, 3)$, so we will first handle the $x$-coordinate and then the $y$-coordinate.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Step 1: Move } 4 \text{ units to the } \underline{\hspace{1cm}} \text{ along the } x\text{-axis}
partial_example.description_latex: The $x$-coordinate is $-4$. Because it is negative, which direction should you move — left or right? Complete the step by stating the correct direction and moving 4 units that way.
From where you stopped after the horizontal move, we go 3 units upward because $y = 3$ is positive. This lands us in Quadrant II, where $x$ is negative and $y$ is positive.
Now identify which quadrant the point falls in. Remember: Quadrant II has a negative $x$-coordinate and a positive $y$-coordinate — does that match $(-4, 3)$?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Name the coordinates of a point that is 5 units to the right and 0 units up from the origin.}
\text{In which quadrant does the point } (-2,\, -7) \text{ lie?}
\text{Plot and label the points } P = (4,\, -3) \text{ and } Q = (-1,\, 5). \text{ State the quadrant for each.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: P \text{ is in Quadrant IV};\quad Q \text{ is in Quadrant II}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Point } C \text{ is located at } (-3,\, 4). \text{ If you reflect } C \text{ across the } x\text{-axis, what are the new coordinates?}
\text{A ship is at position } (2, 3) \text{ on a coordinate map. It travels 4 units left and 5 units down. What are its new coordinates?}

common_mistakes: Students often read the ordered pair as $(y, x)$ instead of $(x, y)$, plotting the vertical value first and the horizontal value second. Always remember that the $x$-coordinate comes first — just like $x$ comes before $y$ in the alphabet.

When one coordinate is zero, students sometimes think the point must be at the origin. A point like $(0, 5)$ is on the $y$-axis, not at the origin — only $(0, 0)$ is the origin, and a zero coordinate simply means there is no movement in that direction.

Students frequently make their vertical move starting from the origin instead of from where they landed after the horizontal move. The two moves are sequential, not independent: always finish the $x$-move first, then make the $y$-move from that new position.
untested_variants: Finding the distance between two points on the coordinate plane using the distance formula or even just counting units between two points on the same horizontal or vertical line.
Identifying or plotting points that lie exactly on an axis (such as $(0, -4)$ or $(6, 0)$) and explaining why those points do not belong to any quadrant.
Reading coordinates of a point that has already been plotted on a given graph (the reverse skill of plotting), especially when the grid scale is not 1 unit per tick.
Working with a coordinate plane that uses a non-standard scale, such as each grid square representing 2 units or 0.5 units instead of 1 unit.
